items.q: Why do earthquakes of similar magnitude cause different amounts of damage?
items.correct: Damage depends on depth, distance from population, building quality, ground type (liquefaction risk), time of day, and emergency preparedness — not just magnitude.
items.misconception: Bigger magnitude always means more deaths.
items.random_wrong: All earthquakes of magnitude 7 cause the same damage everywhere.
items.why: Magnitude measures energy; impact depends on context. A magnitude 7 in a poor, dense city kills thousands; the same offshore or in a wealthy area may kill no one — a key development comparison.

items.q: What is the difference between mitigation and adaptation to climate change?
items.correct: Mitigation reduces the causes (cutting greenhouse gas emissions); adaptation reduces the impacts (e.g. building sea walls, drought-resistant crops, early warning systems).
items.misconception: Mitigation and adaptation mean the same thing — both are about stopping climate change.
items.random_wrong: Mitigation is done by HICs and adaptation is done by LICs.
items.why: The distinction is causes vs consequences. Both are needed: mitigation tackles the source; adaptation copes with already-locked-in change. Many LICs adapt because they can't mitigate global emissions alone.

items.q: What is the difference between a four-figure and six-figure grid reference?
items.correct: Four-figure references identify a 1km square (eastings then northings); six-figure references pinpoint a location within that square to 100m accuracy by adding tenths.
items.misconception: Six-figure references are just longer versions of the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Four-figure references are for OS maps and six-figure references are for atlases.
items.why: Accuracy differs by an order of magnitude. Students forget the 'along the corridor and up the stairs' order (eastings before northings) — a common exam error costing easy marks.

items.q: What is the Hubbert curve about?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Peak oil. “Population” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: South America. “Africa” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What % of world lithium is in the Lithium Triangle?
items.why: Exact answer: 60 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
